chapters.title: When the ground falls out from under you
chapters.text:
A loss that knocks you sideways, whether that is a death, a breakup, a pink slip, or anything that flips the script, can leave you feeling unmoored from who you thought you were.

The trickiest part of walking through something that rewires your outlook is that you come out the other side as a stranger to yourself. You may not catch it in the moment, but every chapter we live reshapes us in some way. The catch is, while you are still sorting through what just happened, you are also trying to pin down who you are right now. It is as though the lens you have always looked through has cracked, and the one thing that used to keep you steady, the quiet certainty of self, has slipped out of reach. Rachel Hollis gives that feeling a name, an identity crisis.

Stormy chapters tend to nudge small corners of our personality around. That is not a vanishing act. That is growth.

That sensation can come from something being stripped away, something being kept out of reach, or a quiet decision that what you used to want no longer fits. The plain truth is, whatever happened, you are still you. Tiny pieces of you may have shifted because of the rough patch, but the bedrock of who you are has not moved. No one else can revoke your identity, and the storm you walked through does not get to write the rest of your story.

Identity is just a word for being who you are. And the pen is in your hand.

You are so much bigger than the rough patch you are in or the chapter you have just lived through. Say you have just been handed a scary diagnosis. You are more than a chart on a doctor desk. You are the only one who gets to draw the lines of who you are.

It is worth holding onto the idea that our identities do bend a little with time as we learn and grow. That bending can feel awkward or muddled, but it usually points to growth, and the core values you carry will always remain in place.

We change across the years, and that is the deal with being alive. But that does not mean your identity has been snatched from you. Just stay true to the person you know yourself to be.

chapters.title: Own your hurt and aim the compass toward growth
chapters.text:
When heartbreak lands, the ache can feel like it has taken up residence in every room. You start to drift through a fog, and it almost sets up shop as your new normal, so much so that you cannot recall a version of yourself that felt any different.

Hollis looks back to age 14, when she lost her brother. That was the year she read about the stages of grief that Dr Elisabeth Kubler-Ross mapped out in the 1960s. Denial, anger, depression, bargaining, and acceptance round out the list.

Guilt tends to walk through four stages of its own. Anger, depression, bargaining, and acceptance. Not everyone walks through loss in the same order, though. Learn to claim your pain and handle it in the way that feels right to you.

Back then, she wanted to fast-forward to acceptance, but she also felt tangled up because she could not spot any of those stages inside her own experience. She wondered if the model just did not fit her. What helped was learning to claim her pain by asking how she felt, why she felt that way, and what she could do to start feeling better.

For anyone standing in a rough patch, no matter the flavor of the storm, it helps to draft a plan. It might feel like the last thing you want to do, but it gives you a way to move.

You have to start opening doors to the people around you and find someone who will listen without an agenda. Sometimes the people in your circle are scared of saying the wrong thing to someone in pain, so do not wait for them to speak up. Be brave enough to crack the door first and see how they respond.

Naming your emotions and your fears out loud will help you move. Find someone in your circle who is willing to listen without an opinion.

chapters.title: Cut guilt loose once and for all
chapters.text:
Loss is not always something that lands on you from outside. Sometimes it is the echo of something you did that cost you something or someone. Maybe you stepped out on your partner and they decided to walk, or maybe you wounded a friend badly enough that they no longer want you in their world. Those situations can stir up guilt that gnaws at you if you let it.

Holding on to guilt will hollow you out if you let it. Move through your emotions and remember that one wrong move does not make you a bad person.

We are human, and sometimes we do things we wish we could take back. That does not make you a bad person, and that is worth holding onto. The fact that you feel guilty and ashamed of what you did means you are owning it, and even though that is hard to sit with, it shows you are trying. The person you hurt may never hand you forgiveness, but you have to hand it to yourself first. Clinging to guilt will not rewrite history. What is done is done.

If you are reading this and you are trapped in self-hatred over something you did, hold onto the truth that hatred never heals. Only love can.

A smart way to handle guilt is to pretend the situation is happening to a friend or a family member. What would you say to them. What advice would you give. We tend to be far softer with other people than we are with ourselves, so take your own advice.

What would you say to a friend in your situation. Would you tell them to let go and move on. Be as kind to yourself as you would be to a friend.

It also helps to remember that even if you did something ugly or made a big mistake, that does not wipe out the good in you. Your actions do not have to dictate the whole rest of your story.

chapters.title: Your lens on the world is never set in stone
chapters.text:
Across the years, we all carry beliefs we are certain are true. When you were small, for example, you may have been sure aliens lived on Mars, and you would have bet your lunch on it. As you grow, your belief changes. That is perception in motion.

Each person perception is unique and shaped by their own story.

We sometimes confuse perception with truth, but every single person carries a different perception of the world. Perception is your way of seeing things, and it is not really about facts. Your perception is built from your own experiences and the lessons those experiences taught you to believe. That means that even if you do not see it right now, you are actually holding the pen on your own perception, which is the way you see the world. That is a lot of power.

You have the power to flip a sour perception into something brighter. Perception is not anchored in fact, and that means it is always open to change.

Being able to reshape your reality by simply thinking about things differently lets you reframe a gloomy view into a sunny one. When you weigh how much that could move the needle in your life, the idea is enormous. The snag is, not many people realize they can change their perception if they choose to, and they stay parked in self-limiting ideas they treat as reality. By tilting the camera the other way, you start to feel something powerful. You feel hope.

Learning to shift your perception is useful when you have walked through something tragic or hard, because in that moment your mind is parked in a very dark place. It does take time and willingness. Try to fill your circle with people who carry the perspective you want to grow into. That can be the people you share your days with, or it can be the authors whose books you read. Slowly, you will start to question and challenge the way you see your world.

You need the influence of others, maybe people who are not standing in such a hard place, to remind you that although life sometimes hurts, it can also be pretty amazing.

chapters.title: Why perspective and mindset are not the same tool
chapters.text:
It is easy to swap mindset and perspective, but they are actually quite different beasts. Your perception is your view of the world, that is, how you see it. Your mindset is what you think about it. The overlap is that both can be changed.

Your mindset is what you think about your own perception. In that sense, it is just an opinion, and opinions can be rerouted.

Hollis compares this to the famous scene in Ghost when Patrick Swayze and Demi Moore are working the clay on the pottery wheel. You can do the same kind of shaping with your own mindset, and the results can be wonderful.

There are two main types of mindset, fixed and growth. A growth mindset believes that anything is possible with enough elbow grease, while the tricky one is the fixed mindset. With a fixed mindset, you do not believe change is possible, and as a result you do not even try. Past experiences have shaped that kind of mindset and put a block in place.

You can tell which kind of mindset you carry by thinking about a time you tried something and it did not work out. What did you do. Did you switch tactics and try another route, or did you give up and assume there was no other way. The first response is a growth mindset in action, but the second is fixed.

A fixed mindset is rigid and misses the openings right in front of it. A growth mindset stays open to trying new things. Which mindset do you carry.

If you have a fixed mindset, the good news is that you can reroute it. Again, it takes time, but by challenging your own thoughts you can start to open up to new possibilities. By trying new things, you will see that there are many ways to crack any one problem. Over time, that will shift your mindset toward a brighter outlook. And a brighter outlook is far more useful when dealing with life hardships than a fixed and gloomy one.

chapters.title: Every loss has a money side, so get your house in order
chapters.text:
It may sound a little cold to talk about money in the middle of an emotional storm, but the bottom line is that every crisis in life carries some kind of money cost. It might be medical bills after someone becomes ill and passes away, it might be the bill for a divorce, but there will always be something. You will handle life ups and downs more easily if your finances are in order.

Every crisis in life costs money in some form. Having your finances in order will be one less thing to worry about.

Hollis recalls the time after her brother died, about two weeks after his funeral. The family got a bill from his dentist for some work he had done before he passed. It felt callous, but the truth is that everyone needs to be paid in order to survive. Having your finances in order and feeling a sense of financial freedom lets you focus on your own situation and your own healing. Feeling lost about money and not knowing where to turn will only make things worse.

You are in charge of your own financial situation, no matter how rough it looks. There is always something you can do to make it better, even if that means reaching out for some kind of help. The first step is to sit down and be honest about your situation, looking at where you are and what you have versus what you owe. There is always a solution if you look hard enough.

Be honest about your financial picture and work out a plan to improve it. Also, always have a plan B for the just in case moments.

Once you have done that assessment, and even if things look reasonable, ask yourself what you would do if they got worse, for example if you lost your job. You need a plan B, a back up plan to cover you if something happens. The truth is that none of us know what is going to happen tomorrow or the day after, and the coronavirus pandemic taught us that. Businesses have closed because they did not have a plan for the just in case.

By focusing on getting your finances in order, you put the control in your hands. When you feel in control, it is easier to deal with life problems.

If you need financial guidance, ask for it. Feeling in control of your money will give you the freedom to focus on your own growth and healing.

chapters.title: What the rough patches try to teach you
chapters.text:
Every experience we walk through, good or bad, hands us lessons. The rough ones often hand us the richest and most valuable ones.

It is not a good idea to dig too deep into a pain you are still swimming in. That is a wound that is still bleeding. But past experiences, the ones that have left a scar, can be very valuable. Be willing to look at those experiences when the time feels right, and ask whether you have grown into a better person since then or whether something has shifted inside you that you do not like. Being open to change means being honest with yourself and accepting that every experience shapes us in ways we cannot always see at the time.

Think back over past hardships and look for lessons you can learn. How did the event change you. Is it a positive change or a negative one.

Hollis talks about how during her hardest times she often went to therapy, but she also knows therapy is not an option for everyone. In that case, journaling is a great idea. It lets you write down your thoughts and feelings and start to make sense of them on paper. When you are going through a hard time, it can feel like one big emotion swirling around inside you. The truth is that it is often several different emotions all tangled together. Journaling can help you pull them apart and make sense of them.

Journaling is a very effective way to make sense of your current experiences and emotions. It can also be deeply cathartic.

chapters.title: Aim for joy and pin down your purpose
chapters.text:
Everyone has heard the saying when life gives you lemons, make lemonade, and Hollis believes it. It may sound odd, but she often laughs at funerals. It is not about being disrespectful. It is about knowing that you cannot hide from grief or hard events in life, that they are going to happen, and you can only handle them in whatever way gives you some comfort.

Hard events happen in life, no matter how hard you try to run from them. The best way to handle those situations is to find comfort in ways that suit you. There are no rules.

Look for joy wherever you can, and if it does not seem to show up, try forcing the issue by doing things you know bring you comfort. For example, if you have always loved going for a coffee with a slice of cake and a good book, do more of that. Hollis also suggests keeping a gratitude diary. That means writing down one thing every day that you are grateful for, and keeping a running list of things you enjoy. It can be a place you love visiting, a favorite sweater that feels warm, or a favorite food. Keep adding to that list and review it often to remember all the reasons you have to be grateful.

Keep a list of everything you are grateful for. Review it often and keep adding to it. You will soon see that you have a lot to be thankful for.

One thing many people struggle with after a loss of some kind is purpose. It is easy to feel like you have lost your purpose when your life has done a 360, because it can feel like everything has changed.

The bottom line is that nobody is really in control of life because we cannot predict what is going to happen from one day to the next. At the start of 2020, nobody would have predicted that a few months later we would all be in lockdown. You have not lost control of your life. You have simply been reminded of the lack of control that has always been there.

Feeling like you have lost control of your life is normal. The truth is, you never had control in the first place. Nobody does. Life is full of twists and turns and cannot be predicted.

The best way to deal with this is to make peace with it. Understand that a lack of control can actually be a beautiful thing, because anything can happen, including the good. Make a decision to start again, rebuild what you lost, and try to make it better than before. By doing that, you will soon start to feel that sense of purpose once more.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Hard times in life teach us lessons, but in the middle of them, it is hard to see anything other than the grief and the overwhelming emotions. Did not See That Coming teaches us that although hard times can be overwhelming and unbearable, they do not last forever. From the flames and the ashes of your situation, whatever it may be, you will rise again.

Learning to change your perspective and your mindset is a vital part of dealing with life hardships. You can choose to be dragged down, or you can choose to process your emotions, work through them, and rebuild. It comes down to a decision. Which route will you choose.

A small exercise to try. If you are feeling lost and have no one to turn to, do a quick inventory of the people in your life. Be brave enough to talk to them about how you are feeling. They may be waiting for you to make the first move. Then take a look at your current mindset about life. If you feel it is holding you back, make a conscious effort to surround yourself with people who inspire you. Read some books, join a class, and get out there to soak in new views. Finally, begin writing a journal and include how you feel every single day. Journals are useful tools for growth, and by writing regularly, you can work through anything that is blocking your progress.
